content.full_text:
TO: File                                                    August 6, 1979

FROM: Ted                                                            M-99-183

RE: Local Government Seminar


LAND USE PLANNING

City Council adopts a general plan but the planning commission

must have the opportunity to comment.  The elements of a plan set

up goals which must then be translated into specifics by ordinances.

The Office of Planning and Research and the Department of Housing

set up elements for general law cities.  The OPR guidelines are

advisory whereas the Housing one's are mandatory.  You can

challenge the fact that a mandatory element is missing from a

general plan at any time.  However, other challenges to the

general plan must be made at the time it is adopted.  After the

plan is adopted and challenges resolved, you may attack the

ordinances enacting the specific goals of the plan but you are

limited in your ability to attack the plan itself.  Servel also

must improve the environmental aspects of the plan.  See the

Mendocino case.

There is a requirement that the general plan and zoning

ordinances be consistent but neither has legal precedence over

the other.  A specific plan is rare.  It is a mini-plan for a

small area with a mixture of uses.

The Subdivision Map Act is very detailed and must be con-

sistent with the general plan and zoning ordinances.  The

tentative map is the crucial action.  Final map is ministerial.

Map Act is triggered when you want to subdivide four or more

[more than four?]

Local Government Seminar
August 6, 1979

Page Two

units for sale or refinancing. See 66474 for information regarding
final maps. Abbreviated parcel maps can be used for subdividing
into four or less parcels.

There is an old practice called four by fouring. This is
successive lot splits into four or fewer parcels in order to
circumvent the Map Act. You need legislative cooperation to deny
this application of the law as circumvention. The League thinks
that map approval is quasi-legislative not quasi-judicial.
This affects the standard of court review. Zoning subdivision
map and general plan can be done by initiative.

CEQA
Under SEQRA the city is usually the lead agency but other
state and local agencies may become involved in the comment
process. These may include air and water agencies. Approval of
the map is up to the city but there is incentive to cooperate with
these other agencies because building permits and water connection
permits will ultimately be permitted, needed.

SCHOOLS

The key to the school question is averaged daily attendance.
The Constitution required that the state provide $120 per
student to every district. Subsequently, this figure has been
augmented by statute. The sales tax was established to finance
education. State law provided some equalization of tax revenues
between, for example, all elementary districts within a single
high school district. This was the way in which the state was
able to give money to districts which were having trouble supporting
themselves. This was called the state computational formula. To

Local Government Seminar
August 6, 1979

Page Three

receive this support, the district had to agree to follow the

state computational formula.

Befom Serrano two thirds of San Mateo's school budget was

from property tax as opposed to fifty percent on the average.

Of the total amount of property tax revenues raised, schools got

about fifty-four percent and the rest went to city and the county.

Federal money is available where a federal impact creates special

needs such as in school districts containing military installa-

tions. There is also categorical need money from the federal

government. The average district receives 10% of its funding

from the federal government.

Serrano required more balancing of funding between dis-

tricts. San Francisco had much wealth but many problems. In

response to Serrano, the Legislature moved away from the state

computational formula and attempted to put 87% of all districts

within a narrow range. Proposition 13 is wreaking havoc on the

school financing system.

INSERT A

CITY AND COUNTY FINANCING

Cities receive 10% of the property tax revenues which

constitutes 25% of cities budget. 25% of city funding is from

state and federal revenue sharing or subventions. Sales tax

provides about 15% of revenues. The state collects the sales

tax but it is locally imposed. Of the 6% sales tax, one cent

goes to the city if the sale is in the city. Allocation

between cities has been a problem in situations where one city

has the people and another city has the shopping center. Property

Local Government Seminar
August 6, 1979                                                    Page Four

     tax now comprises only 15 to 20% of city revenues after Proposition
13.  The remainder of city revenues are from miscellaneous local
taxes.  The reduction in property tax revenues means that the
biggest chunk of city revenues is from government agencies which
are beyond the cities control.  Cities nevertheless are for-
tunate to have a broad base of revenue sources.

     Proposition 13 says that special taxes must be approved by
referendum.  However, no one knows what a special tax is.  At
present, all new taxes are subject to challenge.

     Statute currently prohibits local income taxes from being
instituted.  Payroll taxes have been upheld as not being income
taxes.  The League thinks that taxing is a municipal affair and
that the income tax prohibition could  therefore be struck down.
If taxation is a municipal affair, charter cities would have
complete control over taxing power.  In this way they would be
better off than general law cities.  However, the court could
conclude that taxation is not a municipal affair because of its
complexity and the interrelation between cities.

     Some cities adopt a Delmarre charter which incorporates the
general law except for the revenue power.  There is some question
about the relationship between the municipal affairs power and the
two-third vote requirement for taxes.

     Counties depend more on the property tax and intergovernmental
transfers than do cities.  County is an arm of state government
whereas city is intended to provide additional services to the
people.

Local Government seminar
August 6, 1979                                                                 Page Five

        Cities also receive some funding from fines and forfeitures

through the court system -- about $100,000,000 state-wide.

BONDS

        General obligation bonds are essentially dead because

property tax cannot be increased to cover the bonds if necessary.

Now, bonds must be under the $4 limit under Proposition 13.

There is a move afoot to pass a constitutional amendment which

would move bonds outside of Proposition 13 since the voters must

approve all bonds.
                                                                           and
        The distinction between a bond/a tax is a very hazy one.

Assessment bonds are levied against the people who receive a

particular benefit.  Now under the subdivision map act, the

developer is asked to put in improvements and add the cost to

the.sales price of the property.  Issues which will need to be

resolved in the future are the differences between a fee charge

and a tax.  Bonds can be sold by general bid or private

negotiation.  Negotiation bonds are sold to a person who

frequently is the financial consultant on the bond issue.  General

bids cost about 20% than negotiated sales.  A rating is needed for

a bid but may not be needed under a negotiated bond.  Bond counsel

usually works step by step to make sure that you are following all

the required procedures.  We should read a prospectus of a bond

issue for a better understanding of how this process works.  Hodgeman

of O'Melveny and Meyers could give us more information.

Jim Safran of Stone and Youngbird Financial Consultants could also

help us.

Local Government Seminar
August 6, 1979

Page Six

REDEVELOPMENT

See Health and Safety Code §33000. Redevelopment is a
combination of eminent domain and tax increment financing.

The City Council usually acts as the Redevelopment Agency.
First there is a public study of potential redevelopment areas.
This study is usually performed by a planning consultant.
Success of the plan depends on private sector development. The
Project Area Committee (PAC) advises the Redevelopment Agency.
The PAC has built-in conflict because they are from the
impacted area. Under tax increment financing, the tax rate is
frozen. The difference between the frozen rate and the appre-
ciated rate goes to the agency to pay off any debts in the
project area. The debt may consist of services to the devel-
oper in the form of streets, sidewalks, parks, buying condemned
land under market price, etc. Redevelopment bonds financed out of
the tax increments pay for these services. Private land owners
in the project area are encouraged to do certain things to upgrade
the area. You can tell them to either improve or get condemned.
Redevelopment has been very successful. All local decision-making
so no waiting for federal or state government action. Proposition
13 has hurt redevelopment because it freezes the tax rate.
Redevelopment is, however, still booming because costs are less
and energy savings are higher in downtown areas. Bonds based on
tax increment financing will soon start defaulting. They may try
to establish assessment districts in the project area. If bonds
default, the credit rating of California bonds will go down.

Local Government Seminar
August 6, 1979                                                Page Seven


The poor and the far-right have been against redevelopment.

The Montoya legislation made redevelopment more socially res-

ponsible.


CONTRACTING

Much contracting between public agencies to provide services

like fire and water.  Other than mutual water companies, city

investments are limited to large blue chip securities.  Cities

cannot lend their credit.  The city does not have police, the

sheriff provides protection.  They charge dearly for such

services.  Franchise is an exclusive contract.  Thirty to forty

cities own their own power companies.  Cities provide very few

health services.  Most of that is done by counties.  More and

more cities are seeking contract services with private agencies

or joint powers agencies.  Competitive bidding required for public

works in general law cities.  Newspapers and contractors both want

bidding.  Bidding must be done on personal property but there are

large loopholes.  You must take the lowest reasonable bid.  These

terms provide for much discretion in choosing a bid in certain

circumstances but 99% of the time agencies choose the lowest bid.

Personal services usually do not involve bidding.  Capital main-

tenance is not public works.


ADVISORY AGENCIES

Advisory boards and commissions are populated by interested

persons.  Most are temporary in nature.  People who complain about

an issue end up on these boards.  In small and medium sized cities

their recommendations are received seriously.  Advisory committes

Local Government Seminar
August 6, 1979

Page Eight

are appointed on any number of issues.

JOINT POWERS AGENCIES

Frequently, they are service oriented such as hospitals,

sewers, etc.  JPAs can do anything that their member agencies

could do in their own right.  They are financed by member

agencies or by fees.  Many have been formed for the purposes of

self-insuring.  Many involve purchase lease back financing.

This is because there are debt limit restrictions on the cities

which do not apply to JPAs and nonprofit corporations established

by the city.  The City is insulated from creditors of nonprofit

corporations and certain JPAs see the 1941 revenue bond act.


TP:ma

TP:nrw
8/24/79

Insert A

With the state now providing the bulk of revenues to local school districts,

there is no longer any excuse for the state not providing for 100% equity among

school districts.

sections.question_synthetic: This document is not an FPPC advice letter but rather seminar notes on local government topics including land use planning, school financing, city and county financing, bonds, redevelopment, contracting, advisory agencies, and joint powers agencies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is severely degraded/difficult to read with significant portions illegible. No clear question or conclusion sections present. Appears to be seminar notes rather than an FPPC advice letter response. Metadata indicates document ID 79-183 but this is primarily educational/reference material about local government topics, not a legal advice response to a specific inquiry.

embedding.summary: This is a set of handwritten notes from a Local Government Seminar held on August 6, 1979, covering various aspects of California local government law and finance including land use planning, school funding formulas, property tax and revenue systems, bonds, redevelopment agencies, and contracting procedures.
